title: The Calling of the Apostles part 1
scripture_text: One of those days Jesus went out to a mountainside to pray, and spent the night praying to God. When morning came, he called his disciples to him and chose twelve of them, whom he also designated apostles: Simon (whom he named Peter), his brother Andrew, James, John, Philip, Bartholomew, Matthew, Thomas, James son of Alphaeus, Simon who was called the Zealot, Judas son of James, and Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor.
devotional:
I would like to explore this passage of Jesus calling his disciples and designating twelve of them to become Apostles. Like many of the stories in the gospels this is very familiar and because it is familiar it has been reduced to a very familiar application. Let me explain what I mean.

Many apply this passage something like this.

1. Jesus prayed all night before he made an important decision.

2. After he prayed all night he came to his disciples and chose twelve disciples to become Apostles.

3.Before any of us make any important decisions in life we should spend time praying about it.

This application of this passage is completely legitimate and helpful, prayer should be a constant in our lives as it was in Jesus’ life. Luke mentions prayer more than any other gospel, 21 times, 25 times in Acts he refers to prayer. As people whose lives depend on God we should pray when we have to make important decisions; we should be seeking God’s wisdom and strength as we seek to make the right choices in life.

Having said that, I am not too sure if this is the main lesson of this passage. Yes Jesus prayed then He called the twelve, but if we set the passage in it’s context we will discover that this may have been far more than Jesus praying about the calling of the twelve.

As 21 century Christians we are inclined to read the bible, especially the gospels in a dislocated way, which can make it more difficult to place stories in their context. This is not our fault. Our bibles have chapter numbers, verse numbers and many modern versions have headings that tell us what the proceeding verses are about. All these things are helpful and allow us to navigate ourselves around the bible. However, if we were to remove the chapter and verse numbers, and remove the introductory heading, I think this and many other passages would read very differently.

a. He was accused of blasphemy. We are told in Luke 5:21 ‘But the Pharisees and teachers of religious law said to themselves. ‘Who does he think he is? This is blasphemy. Only God can forgive sins.’

John MacArthur says:- ‘Blasphemy was the most heinous crime in Jewish thought, since it was a direct affront to the person of God. They defined three levels of blasphemy. First, one blasphemed God by speaking evil of His law, as Stephen (Acts 6:13) and Paul (Acts 21:27–28) were falsely accused of doing. A more serious form of blasphemy was to slander, speak evil of, or curse God Himself (Lev. 24:10–16; cf. Ex. 20:7). But the ultimate form of blasphemy was to assume the rights and prerogatives of God; to usurp the role of God and act as if one were God. It was this third and most severe type of blasphemy that the scribes and Pharisees accused Jesus of.’ https://www.preceptaustin.org/matthew-9-commentary. Accessed 25/06/2025

b. The religious leaders complained bitterly to Jesus’ disciples.

Luke 5:30.The word Luke uses is gogguzo, which simply means that they were whispering in low tones about him behind his back and expressing discontentment with him.

c. He was challenged from an unexpected source.

Look at Luke 5:33. ‘One day some people said to Jesus ‘John the Baptist’s disciples fast and pray regularly, and so do the disciples of the Pharisees. Why are your disciples always eating and drinking?’

Marks version read like this:- ‘John’s disciples and the Pharisees were fasting; and they came and said to Him, “Why do John’s disciples and the disciples of the Pharisees fast, but Your disciples do not fast? Mark 2:18.

This is strange because John the Baptist and the Pharisees never sang from the same hymn sheet. Yet here they were collaborating in their criticism of Jesus and his disciples.

d. The religious were watching his every move and began to nit-pick.

Luke tells us that ‘One Sabbath day as Jesus was walking through some grain fields, his disciples broke off heads of grain, rubbed off the husks in their hands, and ate the grain.’ The only way they were able to find fault was to nit-pick, to make something important that wasn’t important. They turned a ‘pin head.’ into a point of theological debate, they intensely scrutinised his every move hoping to catch him out.

e. The religious leaders were full of murderous intent.

‘The enemies of Jesus were wild with rage and began to discuss what to do with him.’ Luke 6:11.

We know from Mark 3:6 that they began to plot how they could kill him. The word for rage (anoia) denotes an almost insane: or mindless fury, to the point that these Pharisees were beside themselves.

So it is with this background that we are told that ‘Jesus went up the mountain to pray and he prayed to God all night.’
prayer: Father help me to see the oppositions and trials of life as an opportunity to pray.
html:
The Calling of the Apostles part 1

Study Text: Luke 6:12-16
One of those days Jesus went out to a mountainside to pray, and spent the night praying to God. When morning came, he called his disciples to him and chose twelve of them, whom he also designated apostles: Simon (whom he named Peter), his brother Andrew, James, John, Philip, Bartholomew, Matthew, Thomas, James son of Alphaeus, Simon who was called the Zealot, Judas son of James, and Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor.

I would like to explore this passage of Jesus calling his disciples and designating twelve of them to become Apostles. Like many of the stories in the gospels this is very familiar and because it is familiar it has been reduced to a very familiar application. Let me explain what I mean.

Many apply this passage something like this.

1. Jesus prayed all night before he made an important decision.

2. After he prayed all night he came to his disciples and chose twelve disciples to become Apostles.

3.Before any of us make any important decisions in life we should spend time praying about it.

This application of this passage is completely legitimate and helpful, prayer should be a constant in our lives as it was in Jesus’ life. Luke mentions prayer more than any other gospel, 21 times, 25 times in Acts he refers to prayer. As people whose lives depend on God we should pray when we have to make important decisions; we should be seeking God’s wisdom and strength as we seek to make the right choices in life.

Having said that, I am not too sure if this is the main lesson of this passage. Yes Jesus prayed then He called the twelve, but if we set the passage in it’s context we will discover that this may have been far more than Jesus praying about the calling of the twelve.

As 21 century Christians we are inclined to read the bible, especially the gospels in a dislocated way, which can make it more difficult to place stories in their context. This is not our fault. Our bibles have chapter numbers, verse numbers and many modern versions have headings that tell us what the proceeding verses are about. All these things are helpful and allow us to navigate ourselves around the bible. However, if we were to remove the chapter and verse numbers, and remove the introductory heading, I think this and many other passages would read very differently.

a. He was accused of blasphemy. We are told in Luke 5:21 ‘But the Pharisees and teachers of religious law said to themselves. ‘Who does he think he is? This is blasphemy. Only God can forgive sins.’

John MacArthur says:- ‘Blasphemy was the most heinous crime in Jewish thought, since it was a direct affront to the person of God. They defined three levels of blasphemy. First, one blasphemed God by speaking evil of His law, as Stephen (Acts 6:13) and Paul (Acts 21:27–28) were falsely accused of doing. A more serious form of blasphemy was to slander, speak evil of, or curse God Himself (Lev. 24:10–16; cf. Ex. 20:7). But the ultimate form of blasphemy was to assume the rights and prerogatives of God; to usurp the role of God and act as if one were God. It was this third and most severe type of blasphemy that the scribes and Pharisees accused Jesus of.’ https://www.preceptaustin.org/matthew-9-commentary. Accessed 25/06/2025

b. The religious leaders complained bitterly to Jesus’ disciples.

Luke 5:30.The word Luke uses is gogguzo, which simply means that they were whispering in low tones about him behind his back and expressing discontentment with him.

c. He was challenged from an unexpected source.

Look at Luke 5:33. ‘One day some people said to Jesus ‘John the Baptist’s disciples fast and pray regularly, and so do the disciples of the Pharisees. Why are your disciples always eating and drinking?’

Marks version read like this:- ‘John’s disciples and the Pharisees were fasting; and they came and said to Him, “Why do John’s disciples and the disciples of the Pharisees fast, but Your disciples do not fast? Mark 2:18.

This is strange because John the Baptist and the Pharisees never sang from the same hymn sheet. Yet here they were collaborating in their criticism of Jesus and his disciples.

d. The religious were watching his every move and began to nit-pick.

Luke tells us that ‘One Sabbath day as Jesus was walking through some grain fields, his disciples broke off heads of grain, rubbed off the husks in their hands, and ate the grain.’ The only way they were able to find fault was to nit-pick, to make something important that wasn’t important. They turned a ‘pin head.’ into a point of theological debate, they intensely scrutinised his every move hoping to catch him out.

e. The religious leaders were full of murderous intent.

‘The enemies of Jesus were wild with rage and began to discuss what to do with him.’ Luke 6:11.

We know from Mark 3:6 that they began to plot how they could kill him. The word for rage (anoia) denotes an almost insane: or mindless fury, to the point that these Pharisees were beside themselves.

So it is with this background that we are told that ‘Jesus went up the mountain to pray and he prayed to God all night.’

Prayer: Father help me to see the oppositions and trials of life as an opportunity to pray.
Bible in a year reading plan: - Joshua 11:10-14:15.
